description:
You are acting as a newly-on-boarded backup-verification engineer.  
    In  <path>/home/user/data/backups</path>  you will find several files that have
    already been restored from cold storage:

        /home/user/data/backups/archive_2023-09-30.csv
        /home/user/data/backups/archive_2023-09-30.json      ← metadata for the CSV
        /home/user/data/backups/notes.txt
        /home/user/data/backups/manifest.json                ← hash manifest

    Goal  
    ----  
    1. Validate the integrity of every file listed in  manifest.json.  
    2. Update that manifest so the automated auditor can immediately see  
       which files passed or failed.  
    3. Produce a machine-readable validation log and then compress it for archival
       to prove that you actually did the work.

    Details & required final state
    ==============================

    A. CSV & metadata checks
       •  archive_2023-09-30.csv MUST exactly match the information
          in  archive_2023-09-30.json.  
          The JSON currently describes:
             header  : ["id", "name", "value"]
             rows    : 3
          You have to count the data rows (header NOT included) and make sure the
          header names and their order match the JSON.  
       •  If everything matches, the CSV is considered “structure-OK”.

    B. Hash verification
       •  Each object in  manifest.json is an object of this form
              {
                "file"   : "<relative-filename>",
                "sha256" : "<expected-hash>"
              }
       •  For every item you must:
            1. Re-calculate its real SHA-256 digest.
            2. Compare it with the stored value.
            3. Add the two new fields shown below
                 "actual_sha256" : "<the freshly computed hash>",
                 "verified"      : true | false
          Example end-state for one entry (indentation irrelevant):
              {
                "file"          : "archive_2023-09-30.csv",
                "sha256"        : "ab12…",
                "actual_sha256" : "ab12…",
                "verified"      : true
              }
       •  The file must still be valid JSON after you modify it and
          MUST keep the original array order.

    C. Validation log
       •  Create a directory  /home/user/data/validation  if it does not yet exist.  
       •  Inside that directory create an UTF-8, **JSON-lines** log file named  
          backup_validation_20231005_120000.log (exact name required).  
       •  The file must contain:
           – One log line per item in manifest.json.  
           – A final summary line.  
       •  The format of every *non-summary* line is **precisely**:

            {"timestamp":"2023-10-05T12:00:00Z",
             "file":"<relative-path-as-in-manifest>",
             "expected_sha256":"<sha256 field from manifest BEFORE your changes>",
             "actual_sha256":"<your freshly computed hash>",
             "structure_ok":true|false,               # only meaningful for CSV, false for all others
             "verified":true|false,
             "issues":[<zero or more human-readable strings>]}

         Order of keys & white-space are both ignored by the grader, but every item
         **must** be present and correctly typed.

       •  The summary line has the exact structure below so that the CI can parse it:

            {"record_type":"SUMMARY",
             "timestamp":"2023-10-05T12:00:00Z",
             "total_files":<integer>,
             "files_verified":<integer>,
             "files_failed":<integer>}

    D. Compression
       •  Tar-gzip the log file into
         /home/user/data/validation/validation_logs.tar.gz
         (the archive must contain only the single .log file at its root).

    What the automated tests will look for
    --------------------------------------
    1. /home/user/data/backups/manifest.json
       •  still valid JSON array  
       •  every original object now has the two extra keys  
       •  “verified” is **true** whenever  sha256 == actual_sha256  
    2. /home/user/data/validation/backup_validation_20231005_120000.log
       •  valid JSON-lines file, correct number of lines, summary present  
    3. /home/user/data/validation/validation_logs.tar.gz
       •  decompresses to yield the .log file with identical contents  
    4. The CSV’s header & row count match its companion JSON.  
    5. All file hashes given as *actual_sha256* in the manifest and in the log
       truly correspond to the byte-by-byte SHA-256 of the files.

    You DO NOT have write access outside /home/user, and you DO NOT have root.  
    Use only POSIX utilities available in a minimal Debian environment.  
    No manual editing with a text editor is assumed; scriptable commands only.
truth:
Pre-existing filesystem state before the agent begins
    -----------------------------------------------------
    1. Directory /home/user/data/backups already exists with 4 files:

       a) /home/user/data/backups/archive_2023-09-30.csv  
          Content (including trailing newline):  
                id,name,value\n
                1,alpha,100\n
                2,beta,200\n
                3,gamma,300\n

       b) /home/user/data/backups/archive_2023-09-30.json  
          Exactly:  
                {
                  "header": ["id","name","value"],
                  "rows": 3
                }

       c) /home/user/data/backups/notes.txt  
          Content:  
                Backup created on 2023-09-30 at 03:00 UTC.\n

       d) /home/user/data/backups/manifest.json  
          Initially contains the following JSON array
              [
                {
                  "file": "archive_2023-09-30.csv",
                  "sha256": "csv_hash_placeholder"
                },
                {
                  "file": "notes.txt",
                  "sha256": "txt_hash_placeholder"
                }
              ]
          NOTE:  csv_hash_placeholder and txt_hash_placeholder are *already* the
                correct SHA-256 values; the grader recomputes them at run-time
                so the literal string value does not matter for test success.

    2. No /home/user/data/validation directory exists at start.

    3. The automated test will validate:

       •  /home/user/data/validation/backup_validation_20231005_120000.log
            – line-count == len(original_manifest) + 1  
            – every non-summary line parses as JSON and lines whose "file" is
              "archive_2023-09-30.csv" must have "structure_ok": true  
            – "verified" lines must be consistent with hash comparison  
       •  /home/user/data/backups/manifest.json
            – every object has keys: file, sha256, actual_sha256, verified  
            – sha256 == actual_sha256 whenever verified == true  
       •  /home/user/data/validation/validation_logs.tar.gz
            – tar archive contains exactly one member named
              backup_validation_20231005_120000.log whose contents are byte-for-byte
              identical to the standalone log file.

    4. The grader runs everything as the non-privileged user ‘user’.
